EF-1 WITH MAX WINDS OF 100 MPH. THE TORNADO HIT A BARN WITH DEBRIS LIFTED INTO SEVERAL FIELDS WHICH THE TORNADO TRACKED THROUGH. THE TORNADO TOOK DOWN SEVERAL TREES ALONG THE PATH WHICH FELL IN DIFFERENT DIRECTIONS. THE TORNADO CONTINUED EASTWARD TO VOAK ROAD WHERE IT BLEW DOWN A FENCE AND ANOTHER TREE BEFORE LIFTING.

A POWERFUL LINE OF THUNDERSTORMS BLASTED THROUGH SULLIVAN COUNTY AND SURROUNDING AREAS BETWEEN 320 PM AND 340 PM BLOWING DOWN NUMEROUS TREES. MAXIMUM WINDS WERE ESTIMATED UP TO 95 MPH. THERE WERE SEVERAL LOCATIONS WITH WIDESPREAD BLOWDOWNS OF MAINLY PINE AND SPRUCE TREES. THERE WERE ALSO MANY OTHER LOCATIONS WITH SEVERAL TREES BLOWN DOWN AND/OR SNAPPED. MOST OF THE STRUCTURAL DAMAGE WAS TO TREES FALLING ON HOMES. THERE WAS AN ESTIMATED 20 HOMES AT LEAST THAT SUSTAINED DAMAGE FROM FALLING TREES.

NWS STORM SURVEY TEAM IDENTIFIED AN EF2 TORNADO EXTENDING FROM GRANVILLE TWP TO FRANKLIN TWP. THE TORNADO WAS 250 YARDS WIDE AND TRAVELED FOR ABOUT 5 MILES WITH MAXIMUM WIND SPEEDS ESTIMATED AT 120 MPH. SEVERAL BUILDINGS WERE DAMAGED, OR DESTROYED, INCLUDING HOMES, SMALL BUSINESSES AND A CHURCH.

*** 6 INJ *** A NWS STORM SURVEY TEAM CONFIRMED AN EF2 TORNADO WHICH TRACKED FROM WILKES-BARRE TWP TO BEAR CREEK TWP ON WEDNESDAY EVENING. THE TORNADO WAS 150 YARDS WIDE WITH TOP WIND SPEEDS OF 130 MPH. THE STORM STRUCK A SHOPPING MALL AREA, DESTROYING SEVERAL BUSINESSES, TREES, VEHICLES AND SIGNS. THE TORNADO CROSSED INTERSTATE 81 AND THEN TRACKED FOR NEARLY 3 MILES ACROSS STATE GAME LANDS BEFORE DISSIPATING.
